passages.title: The Benefits of Urban Green Spaces
passages.content:
In recent years, cities around the world have been investing heavily in creating and maintaining green spaces. Parks, community gardens, and rooftop gardens are becoming increasingly common in urban areas. This trend is not merely aesthetic; research shows that green spaces offer numerous benefits to city dwellers.

First, urban green spaces improve mental health. A study conducted by the University of Exeter found that people who spend at least two hours in nature per week report significantly higher levels of well-being. The calming effect of greenery helps reduce stress and anxiety, which are common issues in fast-paced city life.

Second, these spaces contribute to environmental sustainability. Trees and plants absorb carbon dioxide and release oxygen, helping to combat air pollution. They also reduce the 'heat island effect,' where cities become significantly warmer than surrounding rural areas due to concrete and asphalt absorbing heat.

Third, green spaces foster community interaction. Parks and gardens serve as meeting places where people from different backgrounds can connect. Community gardening projects, in particular, bring neighbours together and create a sense of shared purpose.

Despite these benefits, maintaining urban green spaces requires ongoing investment. City governments must balance the costs of maintenance against the long-term social and environmental returns. Nevertheless, the evidence clearly suggests that green spaces are not a luxury but a necessity for healthy, sustainable cities.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of the passage?

options.label: A
options.text: Urban green spaces are expensive to maintain

options.label: B
options.text: Green spaces provide multiple benefits for city residents

options.label: C
options.text: Cities should focus only on economic development

options.label: D
options.text: Rooftop gardens are the most effective green spaces

questions.explanation: The passage presents several benefits of urban green spaces — mental health, environmental, and social — supporting the main idea that they offer multiple advantages.

questions.stem: According to the passage, how much time in nature per week is associated with higher well-being?
questions.explanation: The second paragraph states that people who spend at least two hours in nature per week report higher well-being.

questions.stem: The phrase 'heat island effect' in paragraph 3 most likely refers to:

options.label: A
options.text: Areas that are popular tourist destinations

options.label: B
options.text: Cities being warmer than surrounding areas

options.label: D
options.text: The cooling effect of parks in summer

questions.explanation: The passage explains that the heat island effect occurs 'where cities become significantly warmer than surrounding rural areas due to concrete and asphalt absorbing heat.'

questions.stem: What can be inferred about the author's attitude towards urban green spaces?
questions.explanation: The author concludes that green spaces are 'not a luxury but a necessity for healthy, sustainable cities,' indicating strong support.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a benefit of green spaces?
questions.explanation: The passage mentions mental health, environmental benefits, and community interaction, but does not mention property prices.

passages.title: The Rise of Remote Work
passages.content:
The COVID-19 pandemic fundamentally changed the way millions of people work. What began as a temporary measure to contain the virus has evolved into a permanent shift for many industries. Remote work, once a niche arrangement offered by progressive companies, has become a mainstream expectation.

Proponents of remote work point to several advantages. Employees save time and money on commuting, enjoy greater flexibility in managing their schedules, and often report higher job satisfaction. A Stanford University study found that remote workers were 13% more productive than their office-based counterparts, with fewer sick days and lower turnover rates.

However, the transition has not been without challenges. Many workers struggle with the blurring of boundaries between work and personal life. Without a physical separation between office and home, the working day can easily extend into evenings and weekends. Feelings of isolation and reduced collaboration have also been reported, particularly among younger employees who value informal workplace interactions.

Companies, too, face new difficulties. Managing remote teams requires different leadership skills, with an emphasis on trust and clear communication rather than direct supervision. Some businesses have invested heavily in digital collaboration tools, from video conferencing platforms to project management software.

Looking ahead, most experts predict a hybrid model will dominate. Under this arrangement, employees split their time between home and office, typically spending two to three days in the workplace. This model aims to combine the flexibility of remote work with the social and collaborative benefits of in-person interaction. The key question for companies is not whether to adopt hybrid working, but how to implement it effectively.

questions.stem: What is the main argument of the passage?

options.label: A
options.text: Remote work is a temporary trend that will fade

options.label: B
options.text: Remote work has both benefits and drawbacks, with hybrid models emerging as the solution

options.label: C
options.text: Companies should force all employees to return to the office

options.label: D
options.text: Remote work only benefits employers, not employees

questions.explanation: The passage presents both advantages and challenges of remote work, concluding that hybrid models are the likely future.

questions.stem: According to the Stanford University study, how much more productive were remote workers?
questions.explanation: The second paragraph states remote workers were '13% more productive than their office-based counterparts.'

questions.stem: What can be inferred about younger employees' attitudes towards remote work?

options.label: A
options.text: They prefer it because of flexibility

options.label: B
options.text: They may miss informal workplace interactions

options.label: C
options.text: They are more productive at home

questions.explanation: The passage notes that 'feelings of isolation and reduced collaboration have been reported, particularly among younger employees who value informal workplace interactions.'

questions.stem: In paragraph 4, the word 'emphasis' is closest in meaning to:
questions.explanation: In context, 'emphasis on trust and clear communication' means placing importance on these qualities.

questions.stem: How many days per week do hybrid workers typically spend in the office according to the passage?
questions.explanation: The final paragraph states 'employees split their time between home and office, typically spending two to three days in the workplace.'

questions.stem: What can be inferred about the author's view on the future of work?

options.label: C
options.text: Believes remote work will be fully replaced

options.label: D
options.text: Thinks all workers should return to offices

questions.explanation: The author presents hybrid models as the predicted dominant approach and frames the key question as 'how to implement it effectively,' suggesting support.

passages.title: Should Schools Ban Smartphones?
passages.content:
The debate over smartphone use in schools has intensified in recent years. Several countries, including France and Australia, have introduced partial or complete bans on mobile phones in schools. Proponents argue that removing phones from classrooms improves concentration, reduces cyberbullying, and encourages face-to-face social interaction.

Research supports these claims. A study by the London School of Economics found that schools which banned phones saw a 6.4% improvement in test scores, with the greatest gains among low-performing students. The reasoning is straightforward: when phones are not available, students pay more attention to their teachers and classmates.

Nevertheless, critics of blanket bans raise valid concerns. They argue that smartphones are powerful educational tools when used appropriately. Educational apps provide interactive learning experiences, and phones offer access to online resources that can supplement traditional teaching. Furthermore, many parents rely on phones to stay in contact with their children before and after school.

Some educators advocate for a middle ground. Rather than banning phones outright, they suggest teaching students responsible use as part of the curriculum. Digital literacy — understanding how to manage screen time, evaluate online information, and use technology productively — is itself an essential skill for the twenty-first century.

Ultimately, the effectiveness of any policy depends on how it is implemented. A ban that is inconsistently enforced may be worse than no ban at all. Schools must consider their specific context, consult with parents and students, and develop clear guidelines that balance the risks and benefits of smartphone use in education.

questions.stem: What is the author's main purpose in writing this passage?

options.label: A
options.text: To argue that all schools should ban smartphones immediately

options.label: B
options.text: To present multiple perspectives on school smartphone policies

options.label: C
options.text: To prove that smartphones have no educational value

options.label: D
options.text: To criticise schools that allow phone use

questions.explanation: The passage presents arguments for and against bans, then suggests a middle ground approach, showing the author's balanced perspective.

questions.stem: According to the London School of Economics study, how much did test scores improve after phone bans?
questions.explanation: The study found a '6.4% improvement in test scores' after schools banned phones.

questions.stem: What does the author imply about blanket phone bans?

options.label: A
options.text: They are always the best solution

options.label: B
options.text: They may ignore the educational potential of smartphones

options.label: D
options.text: They are supported by all parents

questions.explanation: The author presents critics' arguments that 'smartphones are powerful educational tools when used appropriately,' implying blanket bans may be too restrictive.

questions.stem: The word 'supplement' in paragraph 3 most nearly means:
questions.explanation: 'Supplement traditional teaching' means to add to or enhance it, not replace it.

questions.stem: Which word best describes the tone of the passage?
questions.explanation: The author presents multiple viewpoints and concludes with context-dependent advice, maintaining a balanced analytical tone.

passages.title: Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare
passages.content:
Artificial intelligence is poised to revolutionise healthcare in ways that were unimaginable just a decade ago. From diagnosing diseases to discovering new drugs, AI systems are increasingly matching or exceeding human performance in specific medical tasks. However, the integration of AI into clinical practice raises profound ethical and practical questions that society must address.

One of the most promising applications is in medical imaging. Deep learning algorithms can analyse X-rays, MRIs, and CT scans with remarkable accuracy, often detecting abnormalities that human radiologists might miss. A landmark study published in The Lancet demonstrated that an AI system detected breast cancer from mammograms with 94.5% accuracy, compared to 88.0% for human specialists. Moreover, AI can process hundreds of images in the time it takes a human to review a dozen, potentially reducing waiting times for diagnosis.

Drug discovery represents another frontier. Traditional drug development is notoriously slow and expensive, taking an average of ten years and over one billion dollars to bring a new medicine to market. AI can accelerate this process by screening millions of chemical compounds in silico, predicting their effectiveness and safety before laboratory testing begins. During the COVID-19 pandemic, AI systems helped identify existing drugs that could be repurposed for treatment, saving precious time.

Despite these advances, significant obstacles remain. AI systems require vast amounts of high-quality training data, which is not always available, particularly for rare diseases. There are also concerns about algorithmic bias: if training data predominantly represents certain demographics, the AI may perform poorly on patients from other backgrounds. Furthermore, the 'black box' nature of many AI systems — where even their creators cannot fully explain how decisions are reached — poses challenges for medical accountability.

The question of liability is particularly thorny. If an AI system makes a diagnostic error that leads to patient harm, who is responsible? The hospital that deployed the system? The developers who created it? The doctor who relied on its recommendation? Current legal frameworks are ill-equipped to handle such scenarios.

Looking forward, the most likely path is not AI replacing doctors but rather augmenting them. Just as a pilot relies on autopilot systems while remaining ultimately responsible for the aircraft, physicians will increasingly work alongside AI tools that handle routine analytical tasks, freeing them to focus on the human aspects of medicine: empathy, communication, and complex decision-making that requires contextual understanding.

questions.stem: What is the central argument of the passage?

options.label: A
options.text: AI will completely replace human doctors within ten years

options.label: B
options.text: AI offers significant benefits in healthcare but faces ethical and practical challenges

options.label: C
options.text: AI in healthcare is too risky and should be abandoned

options.label: D
options.text: Medical imaging is the only useful application of AI

questions.explanation: The passage presents both the promising applications and the challenges of AI in healthcare, concluding that AI will augment rather than replace doctors.

questions.stem: What accuracy did the AI system achieve in detecting breast cancer from mammograms?
questions.explanation: The Lancet study showed AI detected breast cancer with '94.5% accuracy, compared to 88.0% for human specialists.'

questions.stem: What does the author imply about the current legal system's ability to handle AI-related medical errors?

options.label: C
options.text: It has been specifically designed for AI

questions.explanation: The author states that 'current legal frameworks are ill-equipped to handle such scenarios,' implying inadequacy.

questions.stem: In paragraph 3, the phrase 'in silico' most likely means:
questions.explanation: Context suggests 'in silico' describes computer-based screening of chemical compounds before physical lab testing. 'In silico' is a term meaning performed on computer or via computer simulation.

questions.stem: The author's reference to a pilot and autopilot in the final paragraph serves to:

options.label: B
options.text: Illustrate how AI can assist human professionals without replacing them

options.label: C
options.text: Argue that autopilot technology is unreliable

options.label: D
options.text: Suggest that doctors should be replaced by AI

questions.explanation: The author uses the pilot-autopilot analogy to show that AI will 'augment' rather than replace doctors, similar to how autopilots assist pilots.

questions.stem: Which of the following is mentioned as a challenge for AI in healthcare?

options.label: A
options.text: AI systems are too expensive to develop

options.label: B
options.text: AI systems require large amounts of training data

options.label: C
options.text: Patients refuse to trust AI diagnoses

questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 states that 'AI systems require vast amounts of high-quality training data, which is not always available.'

passages.title: Why Students Should Travel
passages.content:
Many young people dream of travelling the world, but some parents worry that travel will distract from studies. However, travel can actually be a valuable form of education that complements classroom learning.

First, travel broadens horizons. When students visit new places, they encounter different cultures, languages, and ways of life. This exposure develops open-mindedness and cultural sensitivity — qualities that are increasingly important in our globalised world. A student who has experienced life in another country will have a deeper understanding of global issues.

Second, travel builds independence. Navigating unfamiliar cities, managing a budget, and communicating in a foreign language all require problem-solving skills and confidence. These are life skills that cannot be taught from a textbook. Many students return from trips feeling more self-reliant and mature.

Third, travel creates lasting memories and inspiration. Seeing the Northern Lights, walking through ancient ruins, or trying new foods can spark passions that shape a person's future. Many artists, writers, and scientists credit their travels with inspiring their greatest work.

Of course, travel should be planned carefully. Students should consider their academic commitments and budget. Even short trips during school holidays can provide meaningful experiences. The key is to see travel not as an escape from learning, but as another way of learning about the world and ourselves.

questions.stem: What is the main message of the passage?

options.label: A
options.text: Students should travel instead of going to school

options.label: B
options.text: Travel can be a valuable educational experience

options.label: C
options.text: Only long trips abroad are worthwhile

options.label: D
options.text: Parents are right to worry about travel

questions.explanation: The passage argues that travel 'complements classroom learning' and offers educational benefits.

questions.stem: According to the passage, which quality does travel develop through exposure to different cultures?
questions.explanation: The second paragraph states travel 'develops open-mindedness and cultural sensitivity.'

questions.stem: Which group is least likely to benefit from travel according to the passage?

options.label: C
options.text: Students who never leave their home country

questions.explanation: The author presents travel as 'a valuable form of education,' implying those who never travel miss these benefits.

questions.stem: In paragraph 2, the term 'broadens horizons' most likely means:
questions.explanation: The phrase is used metaphorically to mean expanding one's understanding and perspective.
